title: A Super El Niño Looks Likely This Year | Council on Foreign Relations
gist: NOAA has confirmed El Niño is already underway with a two-in-three chance of a Super El Niño (peak Nov 2026–Jan 2027), which compounded with strained food and energy systems from the US-Iran war would amplify already record temperatures; at current 545,000 annual heat deaths and a projected $2T/year economic loss from heat by 2030, immediate action on heat early-warning systems and cooling infrastructure is urgent while the US federal preparedness apparatus is being dismantled.

insights.claim: NOAA has confirmed El Niño is already underway with a two-in-three chance it will grow into a 'Super El Niño' (sea surface temperatures 2.0°C+ above historical average) between November 2026 and January 2027—following eleven consecutive hottest years on record and coming at a time when food and energy systems are already strained from the US-Iran war.
insights.speaker: Alice C. Hill
insights.so_what: A Super El Niño in H2 2026 compounding the Iran-war energy/food shock creates a material tail risk for global supply chains, agricultural prices, and insurance losses—especially in tropical/developing economies.
insights.quote: two-in-three chance that this pattern will grow very strong—into what is known as a 'Super El Niño'—between November 2026 and January 2027
insights.locator: The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) has confirmed

insights.claim: Extreme heat already kills more than 545,000 people annually—a figure considered very conservative due to data gaps—and is the deadliest climate hazard, killing more than floods, fires, and hurricanes combined; without significant adaptation, heat is expected to cost the global economy more than $2 trillion annually by 2030 through lost productivity.
insights.speaker: Alice C. Hill
insights.so_what: The $2T/year heat productivity loss estimate by 2030 is an investment-grade macro risk; sectors exposed to outdoor labor (construction, agriculture, logistics) and cooling demand (HVAC, energy) are directly affected.
insights.quote: Without significant adaptation, heat is expected to cost the global economy more than $2 trillion annually by 2030 through lost productivity.
insights.locator: Extreme heat is the deadliest climate hazard

insights.claim: The 1997–98 El Niño caused $5.7 trillion in global income losses according to a 2023 Science paper, with consequences particularly severe in poorer tropical regions—suggesting the coming Super El Niño could generate losses of comparable or greater magnitude given baseline warming.
insights.speaker: Alice C. Hill
insights.so_what: The $5.7T benchmark for the 1997-98 El Niño provides a lower-bound reference for potential 2026-27 impacts given higher baseline temperatures; emerging market sovereign and corporate credit in tropical regions is at elevated risk.
insights.quote: a paper published in the journal Science in 2023 showing that the 1997–98 El Niño caused $5.7 trillion in global income losses
insights.locator: Studies have found that the economic damage from El Niño

insights.claim: France introduced a Heat Early Warning System after the 2003 heatwave that killed tens of thousands; that HEWS achieved 68% lower deaths than expected during a major subsequent heatwave—demonstrating that well-designed early warning systems have proven, large-scale mortality impact.
insights.speaker: Alice C. Hill
insights.so_what: HEWS are among the highest-ROI public health investments; governments in heat-exposed markets that lack national heat warning systems are carrying preventable mortality risk that can be substantially reduced at modest cost.
insights.quote: the government introduced a HEWS that helped achieve 68 percent lower deaths than expected during a major heatwave three years later

insights.claim: Under the Trump administration, US federal heat preparedness is actively eroding: proposed and enacted cuts to NOAA research capacity, sidelining of permanent federal outdoor worker heat regulations, shrinkage of city adaptation grants, and cancellation of NIHHIS Centers of Excellence—dismantling the 2024-2030 National Heat Strategy framework.
insights.speaker: Alice C. Hill
insights.so_what: US federal retreat from heat preparedness increases downside risk for the 2026–2027 El Niño cycle; US cities and states relying on federal infrastructure will be more vulnerable, increasing local government liability exposure.
insights.locator: Under the Trump administration, this foundation is now eroding.

insights.claim: A parametric heat insurance program launched by HERA and India's Self-Employed Women's Association in 2023 provides automatic payouts to informal economy workers when temperatures cross a set threshold, without waiting for damage assessments—a model that has since expanded to other countries including the United States.
insights.speaker: Alice C. Hill
insights.so_what: Parametric heat insurance for informal workers is a scalable financial inclusion product that addresses both mortality and economic vulnerability; the expansion to the US signals a growing commercial market for climate parametric insurance.
insights.locator: a parametric heat insurance program launched in a partnership between the non-governmental organization HERA

insights.claim: Only a handful of countries have developed national strategies to address heat as a systemic health and economic risk; the UK's National Heat Risk Commission is cited as a leading example, while hundreds of cities have heat action plans without national coordination frameworks in most countries.
insights.speaker: Alice C. Hill
insights.so_what: The gap between city-level adaptation and national heat governance is a policy risk that translates into economic and public health exposure—countries without national frameworks will underperform on heat mortality metrics during the coming El Niño.
insights.locator: few governments have established comprehensive national frameworks
